satire_attempt: The senior engineer, having internalised BG 2.67, blocks Slack at the DNS layer and announces in the team standup that her prajñā is now pratiṣṭhitā. Her LinkedIn bio reads: 'Staff Engineer | The wind does not enter my sail | DM closed for sense-control reasons.' She has not shipped in three weeks. Her PR queue has 47 pending reviews from teammates whose own prajñā is being eroded by waiting on her. When her manager asks about the lock-ordering refactor, she replies 'I am holding the architectural model in working memory; please do not deflect the boat'. The team has unshipped a feature in her honor.
verdict_reason: My satire trades on the analog's pretentious-sounding Sanskrit terms (prajñā, pratiṣṭhitā) and on a hypothetical-bad-implementation of the verse where the engineer takes 'restrain the wind' to mean 'go full hermit'. But this exact failure mode is what the falsifiability clause in the verse pre-empts: 'an on-call engineer who silences PagerDuty in the name of sense-control has misread the verse entirely. Restraint is a yoke on the mind's drift, not a wall around the operator.' My LinkedIn-tier hermit-engineer is precisely the figure the verse names as the misreader. The satire borrows the verse's structure to mock its bad reception; the verse itself is undamaged. The counter-example also explicitly identifies in-task input (incident dashboards, current PR review) as not-the-verse, which closes the other escape route my satire might have used. Per calibration: 'satire trades on the analog's earnestness without undermining the claim' ⇒ PASS.
